text: remarked 14 This is the conceptual discussion entitled Individuals and Groups which precedes the six following chapters that each deal with a cross-cultural examination of ritual passages as they pertain to the life cycle, from pregnancy to death. In other words, van Gennep posits gift-giving as a foundational principle at play in ritual phenomena, tying individuals and groups together. Liminality and the Modern 64 upon this similarity himself. In a 1905 review of a journal issue of L’Année Sociologique, van Gennep asks himself with genuine surprise: why it is that his own work, though so essentially similar to Mauss’s, is so openly criticized in L’Année Sociologique, while Mauss’s was obviously accepted: ‘Mauss posed some rules of method which are like those which I presented here (one nearly reproached me for them) ... which I applied in Tabou et Totémisme à Madagascar, formulated with more precision in my Notes sur le Totémisme ...’ (van Gennep [1905] as quoted in Zumwalt 1982: 4). It must also be stressed that neither Mauss’ critique of Rites of Passage, nor van Gennep’s devastating critique of EFRL led to a final rupture between the two thinkers. In fact, in 1913, Mauss positively reviewed van Gennep’s Algerian ethnography (Zumwalt 1982: 4). Mauss praised van Gennep for the incredible ethnographic effort and the analytical precision found in the work. These were exactly what van Gennep had pointed out as fatal weaknesses in Durkheim’s EFRL, published within weeks. In fact, we know with certainty that while on the surface the split between van Gennep and the Durkheimians had become irrevocable, the bridges between van Gennep and Mauss were never totally burnt. 15 During World War I, when van Gennep realized that he would sooner or later leave Switzerland, he could find only one person to contact in order to help him back into French academia: Marcel Mauss. The exchange that followed provides one little glimpse into Durkheim’s role in keeping van Gennep out of French academia. Arnold van Gennep’s Fatal ‘Encounter’ with Durkheim via Mauss in 1915 In 1915 van Gennep attempted to re-enter French academia, and he did so initially via Mauss. While we do not have access to the letter(s) which van Gennep initially directed to Mauss, the main point relating to the content can be inferred from the letters Durkheim later wrote to Mauss, and which we fortunately do have access to. In this extremely delicate situation, in the middle of World War I, van Gennep had asked for Mauss’ support, and he quite evidently also asked Mauss if it would be proper of him to contact Durkheim directly. Durkheim had by then established an enormous power position concerning employment within the social sciences; it would be hard to get anywhere without at least his consent. And Mauss apparently told van Gennep to go ahead, and that he had his recommendations. There is of course also another hypothetical scenario (and one does not exclude the other, quite the contrary): that Mauss and van Gennep had talked very openly 15 As late as the 1930s, when Mauss had become Chair at the Collège, van Gennep was still fighting to secure some kind of stable income for his old age. Van Gennep would seek Mauss’ support for the creation of a Chair in French and comparative folklore at the Collège or solicit his help towards a position as director of studies in religion or folklore (Fournier 2006: 300). It was again to no avail, but the fact that he even tried again shows that their relationship was more complex than it may seem at a first glance. Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 65 about the employment situation in France as they met during the conference in Neuchâtel in 1914. Mauss had come to the conference on van Gennep’s invitation. As old colleagues it is likely that they did talk together. This is all the more plausible, given the timing and the general context: the declared aim of the whole conference was to lay out the framework for the establishment of the ethnographic and social sciences, including not only epistemological and methodological issues, but indeed also the content and institutional form that new university degrees in the social sciences should take. Mauss was working on the very same issues in France; and Mauss and van Gennep converged in essential ways concerning their discipline, not least its methodology. The conference took place just weeks before the outbreak of World War I. The war was in the air, and van Gennep may have had a presentiment that his permanence in Switzerland might be in jeopardy. Or maybe he quite simply missed France, and hoped to come back. We know that Van Gennep, after having sought the consent of Mauss, made contact with Durkheim during the spring of 1915; he was only formally expelled from Switzerland during the fall of that year. There can be little doubt that Van Gennep still – after all – nurtured ambitions to eventually enter French academia. His applications to the Collége had come to nothing, but his ambitions had not evaporated. With a permanent position and at a distance from French academic power struggles, he may now have felt more comfortable attempting to re-launch himself, despite the fact that he and Mauss had, just a few years before, been competing for the same jobs in Paris. And there is safe ground to assume that van Gennep, despite everything, continued to think highly of Mauss. Van Gennep may have felt that he by now represented even less of a threat to Durkheim and his followers, whose power and influence had by then become very hegemonic. If this is so, he was wrong. Durkheim wrote two letters to Mauss with van Gennep as a topic, the first in April–May 1915 (precise date not legible), the second on 1 June 1915 (Durkheim 1998: 455–7; 461–2). The wording in the first letter deserves to be quoted in full: Received a letter from Van Gennep who presents his candidature for
